title: Wait, Teachers Are Actually Earning LESS Now? Here's Why
summary: Imagine getting a raise at your job, but then finding out you can actually buy less stuff with that money. That's what's happening to teachers across the United States. A new report from the National Education Association (NEA), the country's biggest teachers union, says the average public school teacher now makes $74,495 a year. That's 3.5% more than last year. But here's the twist: because of inflation—when prices for things like food and rent go up—teachers' real earnings have dropped nearly 5% since 2017. In other words, they're making less money in terms of what it can actually buy. The report also found big differences between states. Teachers in California earn the most, around $103,552 on average, while Mississippi teachers earn just $54,975. But living in California costs a lot more, so the gap isn't as simple as it looks. Starting salaries are also all over the map: Washington, D.C. offers the highest at $64,640, while Montana starts teachers at only $36,682. Support staff like bus drivers and cafeteria workers are struggling too—their pay has dropped by $2,344 since 2016 after adjusting for inflation. NEA President Becky Pringle said teachers deserve pay that reflects their expertise and the essential role they play. One bright spot? Washington state saw a 36% real increase in teacher pay after its supreme court ordered better school funding, even fining the state $100,000 a day until it complied. So next time you see your teacher, remember: they're working hard even when the numbers don't add up in their favor.
why_it_matters: Your teachers work hard to help you learn, and when they are paid fairly, they can focus more on making school a great place.

keywords.explanation: When prices for things like food and rent rise, so money buys less than before.
A large group of workers who join together to protect their rights and pay.
The amount of money you earn after considering how much prices have gone up.
To follow a rule or order, like a court’s decision.
Making someone pay money as punishment for not following a rule.
Special skills and deep knowledge that someone has from studying or doing a job for a long time.

questions.question: What is the average salary for U.S. public school teachers according to the NEA?
Since 2017, how much have teachers’ real earnings dropped?
Which state has the highest average teacher pay?
Where do starting teachers earn the most?
Who is the president of the NEA?
What happened in Washington state to improve teacher pay?
options: The state supreme court ordered better funding and fined the state $100,000 a day until it obeyed.
Teachers went on strike for a whole year.
The federal government gave extra money to all Washington schools.
Local businesses donated millions to teacher salaries.
questions.correct_answer: The state supreme court ordered better funding and fined the state $100,000 a day until it obeyed.

background_read: In the U.S., teachers are paid by state and local governments, so salaries can be very different depending on where you live.
Inflation means the cost of housing, food, and other needs goes up over time; even if you get a raise, you might not be able to buy as much.
The NEA (the National Education Association, the country’s largest teachers union) fights for better pay and working conditions for educators.
Article_Structure: WHO: Public school teachers and support staff across the United States
WHAT: A report shows teacher pay rose 3.5%, but after inflation, real earnings fell nearly 5% since 2017
WHERE: 50 states and Washington, D.C.; California pays the most, Montana starts the lowest
WHY: Inflation eats into salary increases, and many states don’t put enough money into school funding

perspectives.description: They believe pay should match the skills and importance of their job. Low pay can push people away from becoming teachers.
Some states say they don’t have enough tax money to raise pay quickly, while others order courts to force them to increase school funding.
When teachers are underpaid, schools may struggle to keep good teachers, which can affect the quality of education in a community.
